APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses zero-resource neural machine translation, a core NLP/ML topic and well within top-tier venue scope. The methodology (teacher-student framework with sentence- and word-level KL objectives) and comparisons against strong baselines (pivot-based, multilingual, transfer learning, and direct NMT) use standard datasets (Europarl, WMT) and evaluation metrics (BLEU, KL), matching community expectations. The problem formulation and evaluation are appropriate for the audience, and the paper is positioned correctly within the NLP/ML research community despite some noted practical limitations (beam cost, sampling trade-offs).

ORIGINALITY.justification: The literature context shows identical prior papers with the same title and approach (items [1] and [2]), indicating the core teacher-student zero-resource framework is not new. Related work ([3],[4]) already explores pivot-based and probabilistic training objectives, so the paper appears to largely replicate existing ideas rather than introduce a genuinely new paradigm. While some implementation details (e.g., specific sentence-/word-level KL instantiations and empirical comparisons of beam/greedy/sampling approximations) may add incremental nuance, they do not constitute a significant novel contribution beyond the cited prior work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments showing BLEU improvements over pivot-based baselines and comparable-size direct NMT, and the teacher-student KL-based objectives are mathematically plausible. The summary indicates correct use of teacher (pivot→target) and student (source→target) models and both sentence- and word-level KL objectives, and reasonable baselines (pivot method, multilingual, transfer learning, MLE) are compared. However, important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameters reported, no mention of statistical significance testing) and the authors note limitations of beam-cost and Monte Carlo sampling that leave trade-offs unresolved. These minor methodological gaps prevent a perfect technical score but do not invalidate the main conclusions.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important practical problem of zero-resource NMT by proposing a teacher-student framework that trains source-to-target models using only source-pivot parallel data, which is a widely relevant setting. The reported empirical gains (+3.0 BLEU over pivot-based baselines and occasional parity with direct NMT trained on comparable-size data) indicate the approach could change how researchers handle absent direct parallel corpora. The introduction of sentence- and word-level KL-based teaching objectives provides concrete new techniques that are likely to stimulate follow-up work and be adopted in related low-resource and cross-lingual transfer research. While not clearly transformative, the contribution is a strong, broadly useful advance for the subfield and adjacent areas.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides a reasonable set of experiments (multiple datasets: Europarl, WMT, a trilingual dev set) and compares against appropriate baselines (pivot-based, multilingual, transfer learning, and standard NMT), and it evaluates both sentence- and word-level objectives — supporting the main claims. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code) and sensitivity/ablation analyses are limited beyond beam-size and sampling trade-offs; more extensive ablations (e.g., effect of training data size, hyperparameter sensitivity) would strengthen the empirical case. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper situates itself relative to other pivot and zero-resource approaches (cf. joint pivot training and expected-likelihood methods), and limitations are acknowledged, but the experimental depth is adequate rather than comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation of limitations and evaluation metrics is explicit, which aids clarity. However, important reproducibility/presentation details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables/examples effectively support the text, so minor presentation improvements are needed.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (pivot-based Cheng et al. 2016, multilingual/many-to-one Firat et al. 2016b, transfer learning Zoph et al. 2016, and standard NMT) and uses standard metrics (BLEU, KL) on relevant datasets (Europarl, WMT). It reports clear improvements over pivot baselines and discusses limitations and trade-offs, and the related-work coverage is described as thorough (so relevant competing approaches such as joint pivot training and related zero-resource objectives are cited). However, some practical fairness details are missing (no hyperparameters or code reported), which hampers reproducibility and full assessment of fair comparison, and it is not explicit whether all closely related methods (e.g., joint training or maximum expected likelihood approaches) were quantitatively compared rather than only discussed.

main_claims: Introduce a teacher-student framework that trains a source-to-target NMT model (student) guided by a pivot-to-target NMT model (teacher) using only a source-pivot parallel corpus (zero-resource setting).
Propose both sentence-level and word-level teaching objectives (including a KL-divergence based word-level objective) to transfer knowledge from teacher to student.

Show empirical improvements over pivot-based baselines (reporting +3.0 BLEU on various language pairs) and in some settings slightly outperform a standard NMT trained on comparable-size direct parallel data.
_summary.methodology: Train a pivot-to-target NMT teacher on pivot-target parallel data, then optimize a source-to-target student on source-pivot parallel data by minimizing KL divergence between teacher and student outputs either at the sentence level (mode/beam/greedy approximations) or at the word level (word-wise KL summed over positions).
datasets: shared task 2006 aligned trilingual development set (2000 sentences)
baselines: Multilingual/one-to-one and many-to-one methods (Firat et al., 2016b)
limitations: Sentence-level beam approximation improves performance but increases time complexity linearly with beam size.
Word-level Monte Carlo sampling yields higher KL divergence (poorer KLD) though it may introduce beneficial target-side diversity; trade-offs are not fully resolved.
Further evaluation (e.g., of low-resource combination methods) is left for future work.
